key_insight: The paper enables analytical nuclear forces for excited states computed with the Bethe-Salpeter equation, making large-scale excited-state relaxation calculations practical for solids.
engineering_relevance: This gives materials engineers a scalable way to predict defect relaxation and vibronic effects in realistic heterogeneous materials beyond semilocal TDDFT accuracy.
method: Plane-wave BSE force formulation using density-matrix perturbation theory and a Lagrangian approach
Avoids explicit empty-state summations and per-displacement response calculations
GPU acceleration extends feasibility to systems with hundreds of atoms
evidence_signal: Validated on two defect systems: the NV center in diamond and a carbon-dimer defect in h-BN, showing agreement with TDDFT in one case and improved relaxation physics in the other.
watch_item: The approach is computationally advanced and likely depends on careful implementation of screening, convergence, and GPU-enabled infrastructure for robust production use.
paper_title: Analytical Forces from the Bethe-Salpeter Equation for Large-Scale Excited-State Relaxation
